definition: The most common polite greeting when you meet someone.
example: 안녕하세요, 처음 뵙겠습니다 (Hello, nice to meet you)
usage: Use when greeting someone, especially the first time or in formal settings.

definition: You stay; the other person is leaving. Literally “go in peace.”
example: 수고하셨습니다. 안녕히 가세요 (Good work. Goodbye.)
usage: Say to a guest or customer when they leave your place or office.

definition: You leave; the other person stays. Literally “stay in peace.”
example: 먼저 가 보겠습니다. 안녕히 계세요 (I’ll go first. Goodbye.)
usage: Say when you leave someone’s home, office, or shop.

definition: A set phrase when you meet someone for the first time.
example: 처음 뵙겠습니다. 만나서 반갑습니다 (Nice to meet you. Pleased to meet you.)
usage: Use right after introducing yourself in a formal or polite situation.

definition: Expresses that you are glad to meet the other person.
example: 이렇게 뵙게 되어 반갑습니다 (I’m pleased we could meet like this.)
usage: Often paired with 처음 뵙겠습니다 or right after an introduction.

definition: Polite thanks; appropriate with strangers, elders, or in service settings.
example: 도와주셔서 감사합니다 (Thank you for helping me.)
usage: Use whenever you receive help, a gift, or good service.

definition: Polite thanks; slightly softer tone than 감사합니다 in many situations.
example: 선물 고맙습니다 (Thank you for the gift.)
usage: Use with friends, colleagues, or when thanks feel personal.

definition: A polite apology for mistakes, lateness, or inconvenience.
example: 늦어서 죄송합니다 (I’m sorry I’m late.)
usage: Prefer this in formal settings; common in shops and on the phone.

definition: An apology; polite ending -합니다.
example: 제 실수입니다. 미안합니다 (It’s my mistake. I’m sorry.)
usage: Use among peers or when a softer apology than 죄송합니다 fits.

meaning: It’s okay / You’re welcome / I’m fine
definition: Declines worry, accepts an apology, or says “no problem.”
example: 괜찮습니다, 걱정하지 마세요 (It’s okay. Don’t worry.)
usage: Reply when someone apologizes or asks if you are all right.

meaning: I will eat well (before a meal)
definition: Said before eating to thank the cook or host.
example: 맛있게 준비해 주셔서 감사합니다. 잘 먹겠습니다 (Thank you for preparing this. I’ll enjoy it.)
usage: Say when food is served, before you pick up chopsticks.

meaning: Thank you for the meal (after eating)
definition: Said after finishing to thank the host or restaurant.
example: 정말 맛있었습니다. 잘 먹었습니다 (It was delicious. Thank you for the meal.)
usage: Say when you leave the table or pay at a casual restaurant.

definition: Praise for food; polite speech level (-요).
example: 이 음식 정말 맛있어요 (This dish is really delicious.)
usage: Say while eating or to the cook to show appreciation.

definition: Asks the price of something.
example: 이거 얼마예요? (How much is this?)
usage: Use in shops, markets, or cafés when asking for the price.

definition: Asks about a place or location (polite).
example: 화장실이 어디예요? (Where is the restroom?)
usage: Use when you need directions in polite speech.

definition: Asks the current time.
example: 지금 몇 시예요? (What time is it now?)
usage: Use on the street, in class, or when confirming a schedule.

definition: Asks what something is (polite).
example: 이 음식 이름이 뭐예요? (What is the name of this dish?)
usage: Use when you point at or hold something you do not know.

meaning: Who is it? / Who are you?
definition: Asks someone’s identity on the phone or at the door (polite).
example: 여보세요, 누구세요? (Hello? Who is this?)
usage: Common on phone calls; at the door use with caution and politeness.

definition: Agrees or answers “yes” politely.
example: 한국어 공부하세요? 네, 공부해요 (Do you study Korean? Yes, I do.)
usage: Default polite “yes” in conversation and service situations.

definition: Disagrees or answers “no” politely.
example: 아직 안 먹었어요? 아니요, 먹었어요 (Haven’t you eaten yet? No, I have.)
usage: Use for polite negation; note: sometimes 네 is used to mean “what you said isn’t so” in older style—beginners use 아니요 for “no.”

definition: Confirms that something is correct.
example: 지하철역이 여기 맞아요? 네, 맞아요 (Is the subway station here? Yes, that’s right.)
usage: Use when you agree with a fact or direction someone gave.

definition: Says you do not know the answer (polite).
example: 길을 모르겠어요 (I don’t know the way.)
usage: Use when you cannot help with a question or directions.

definition: Shows you understood instructions or information (formal).
example: 내일까지 제출하세요. 알겠습니다 (Submit by tomorrow. Understood.)
usage: Reply to teachers, bosses, or announcements.

definition: A polite request for help.
example: 가방이 무거워요. 도와주세요 (My bag is heavy. Please help me.)
usage: Use in emergencies or when you need a small favor from staff.

definition: Asks the other person to slow down (polite request).
example: 한국어가 어려워요. 천천히 말해 주세요 (Korean is hard for me. Please speak slowly.)
usage: Very useful for learners in real conversation.

definition: Asks for repetition (polite).
example: 잘 못 들었어요. 다시 말해 주세요 (I didn’t catch that. Please say it again.)
usage: Use on the phone or in noisy places.

definition: Asks someone to wait briefly.
example: 잠깐만요, 지갑을 찾을게요 (One moment, I’ll find my wallet.)
usage: Use before paying, answering the door, or checking something.

definition: Expresses love; polite -요 ending.
example: 부모님께 사랑해요라고 했어요 (I said “I love you” to my parents.)
usage: Common in songs and dramas; tone depends on relationship.

definition: Says you want to see someone.
example: 오랜만이에요. 보고 싶었어요 (Long time no see. I missed you.)
usage: Friends, family, or partners; often heard in dramas.

meaning: I like it / I like you
definition: Expresses liking something or someone (polite).
example: 이 노래를 좋아해요 (I like this song.)
usage: Use for hobbies, food, music, or mild romantic interest.
